heading: MODULUS QUINTUSDECIMUS
card_summary: This chunk opens the Fifteenth Module with its first Concentus, titled "De amplo principis Imperio" (On the vast empire of the Prince). Zorzi expounds the universal dominion of Christ the triumphator, drawing on Paul's Epistle to the Hebrews ("quem constituit haeredem universorum") and Isaiah's prophecy of the Prince of Peace ("Multiplicabitur eius imperium, et pacis eius non erit finis"). He explains the current imperfection of that peace — enemies not yet placed beneath his footstool — with another Pauline citation. The section then shifts to the Davidic throne and the royal prerogative of the tribe of Judah, citing Genesis ("Non auferetur sceptrum de Iehuda"), Jacob's blessing, Moses, and the narrative of David's election and anointing by Samuel, his service under Saul, and his archetype-decreed kingship.
essay:
## Overview

Chunk 129 opens the *Modulus Quintusdecimus* with its first *Concentus*, headed "De amplo principis Imperio" — On the Vast Empire of the Prince. The subject is the universal sovereignty of Christ, understood simultaneously through Pauline theology, Isaianic prophecy, and the Kabbalistic-typological significance of the Davidic dynasty and the tribe of Judah. The exposition proceeds by moving from the eschatological fullness of Christ's kingship down through its historical prefigurations in the Hebrew scriptures.

## The Triumph and Universal Dominion of Christ

Zorzi opens with a bold eschatological image: after the triumph, dominion is granted to the triumphator who is *rex regum et Dominus dominantium* (king of kings and lord of lords). This title, drawn from Revelation 19:16 and 1 Timothy 6:15, frames Christ's rule in the language of Roman imperial triumph, a rhetorical device characteristic of Zorzi's synthesis of Christian and classical registers.

The scope of this empire is defined by the Pauline letter to the Hebrews:

> Quem constituit haeredem universorum.

<cite>p. 2694</cite>

"Whom he appointed heir of all things" (Hebrews 1:2). Zorzi's gloss is pointed: this inheritance cannot be extended any further, since all things (*universa*) are already subject to him. The superlative totality is stressed — there is no remainder outside this dominion.

Isaiah's nativity oracle (Isaiah 9:6–7) supplies the prophetic confirmation:

> Multiplicabitur eius imperium, et pacis eius non erit finis, quia Princeps pacis erit.

<cite>p. 2694</cite>

"His empire shall be multiplied, and there shall be no end of his peace, for he shall be the Prince of Peace." This citation anchors the cosmological theme of harmony — *harmonia mundi* — in specifically messianic terms: the endless increase of dominion is coextensive with peace without limit.

## The Problem of Present Warfare

Zorzi immediately addresses a potential objection: if Christ is the Prince of Peace, why do wars multiply in the lower kingdom? The answer is ecclesiological and eschatological. The Church is currently the *ecclesia militantium* — the church of those still fighting — because enemies have not yet been placed beneath Christ's footstool. A second Pauline citation makes this explicit:

> Sedet in dextera Dei de caetero expectans, donec ponantur inimici eius scabellum pedum ipsius.

<cite>p. 2694–2695</cite>

This is drawn from Hebrews 10:12–13 (echoing Psalm 110:1). The image of Christ seated at the right hand of God, *waiting*, introduces a temporal gap between inaugurated and consummated dominion. Only when death — *inimica mors* — is destroyed will perfect peace be enjoyed in all members of the body. The eschatological reserve is thus built into the very architecture of Zorzi's harmonic scheme: the present dissonance of the *regnum inferius* is a provisional state awaiting resolution.

## The Davidic Throne and the Tribe of Judah

The prophetic text continues — "Super solium David" — and Zorzi uses this pivot to develop a typological meditation on the Davidic kingship. David is identified as the first and uniquely God-beloved king among those who reigned over all Israel, and his descent from Judah is noted as a mark of royal distinction.

Central to this passage is the doctrine of the particular privileges (*privilegia peculiaria*) of each tribe of Israel, disclosed through the blessings of Jacob (Genesis 49) and Moses (Deuteronomy 33). These blessings, Zorzi says, contain an *occultum et profundum mysterium* — a hidden and deep mystery — a standard Kabbalistic qualifier indicating that the plain sense conceals esoteric content.

The royal prerogative of Judah is confirmed by the oracle from Genesis 49:10:

> Non auferetur sceptrum de Iehuda.

<cite>p. 2695</cite>

"The scepter shall not depart from Judah." Zorzi reads Jacob's trust of Judah as surety for Benjamin's journey to Egypt (Genesis 43:9) as a narrative confirmation that Judah's word carries royal authority — the word of a king must be true.

## The Archetype of Kingship and David's Trials

An important metaphysical point follows: even when the visible kingdom did not rest in the tribe of Judah — as during Saul's reign — the scepter was always present *in Archetypo*, in the archetypal realm. This is a Neoplatonic move characteristic of Zorzi: empirical history is secondary to the ideal pattern inscribed in the divine mind (*decretum in mente divina*). David was elected king and anointed by Samuel, but the actual possession of the kingdom was delayed for many years while he served under Saul.

The narrative becomes psychologically nuanced: David's excellence earned him fame among the people but hatred from Saul, so that amid persecutions and traps he preferred to serve the *allophyli* (foreigners, the Philistines) rather than an envious king. The Latin term *allophylis* draws directly on Septuagintal usage and appears as a technical borrowing in Zorzi's exegetical vocabulary. The phrase *livori cedendum* — yielding to envy — characterizes Saul's kingship as one corrupted by the very vice that true royal dignity must transcend.

## Connection to Zorzi's Cosmological Project

This *Concentus* belongs to the Fifteenth Module, which develops the theme of the Prince's empire as a harmonic reality — *imperium* as cosmic *concentus*. The passage weaves together several threads central to *De Harmonia Mundi*: the Pauline-Kabbalistic identification of Christ as universal heir, the eschatological tension between present dissonance and future consonance, the typological reading of the Hebrew tribal blessings as encoding hidden mysteries, and the Neoplatonic doctrine of archetypal kingship preceding its historical instantiation. The movement from prophetic text (Isaiah) through Pauline theology to the Davidic narrative enacts Zorzi's characteristic method of concordance, in which Scripture, philosophy, and esoteric tradition are shown to harmonize in their deeper registers.
